paper_title: Domain Arithmetic: One-Shot VLA Adaptation under Environmental Shifts

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often fail to perform the same learned tasks under environmental shifts, such as changes in camera pose and shifts to a different but similar robot (e.g., from Panda to UR5e). Adapting these models to the shifted environment (i.e., target domain) often requires training on multiple demonstrations for each task, which are costly to collect. To reduce the burden of data curation and training, we propose an analogy-based method that adapts VLA models under environmental shifts through weight vector arithmetic with domain-specific information addition, named Domain ARiThmetic (DART). Unlike prior approaches, DART requires collecting only a single demonstration, enabling efficient adaptation. To accurately isolate domain-specific information for addition, DART performs subspace alignment between singular components in weight vectors to filter out noisy components. In both simulated and real-world experiments, DART outperforms existing VLA adaptation methods in one-shot scenarios across diverse visual and embodiment shifts. Code is available at https://github.com/snumprlab/dart.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models trained on large-scale corpora show strong multi-task capabilities [35, 26, 27, 52, 80]. Despite their success within trained environments, i.e., source domain, VLA models face challenges when deployed in new environments to perform learned tasks, common real-world deployment scenario. These environmental shifts involve altered camera poses, distinct sensor calibrations, or embodiment modifications, leading to substantial performance degradation [13, 15, 33, 65, 74, 77, 79]. Thus, post-hoc adaptation remains essential to guarantee reliable execution in the shifted environment, i.e., target domain. However, existing VLA adaptation approaches [1, 12, 13, 32, 63, 67] often require extensive expert demonstrations for every policy task in the target domain, resulting in severe deployment bottlenecks [42,60,72]. Also, fine-tuning on limited data often fails to generalize to unseen tasks [12, 67]. These authors contributed equally. JC is with ECE, IPAI and ASRI in SNU and corresponding author. 2 T. Kang et al. Fig. 1: One-shot VLA adaptation under environmental shifts. Environmental shifts can cause source-trained VLA policy to fail in the novel target domain. (a) Fulldata fine-tuning adapts successfully but requires task-wise demonstrations, incurring expensive data collection costs. (b) One-shot fine-tuning is data-light but often fails to adapt across tasks. (c) Our one-shot adaptation extracts domain-specific directions from fine-tuned weights and adapts the policy via weight arithmetic. For practical policy deployment, extreme data efficiency for adaptation is essential in settings where collecting task-wise demonstrations at scale is typically infeasible, such as household environments. Thus, we aim for one-shot VLA adaptation where policy adapts under environmental shifts using only single demonstration of single task. To enable this, we leverage the insight that single demonstration can provide transferable domain knowledge. It allows source-trained base VLA model to harness its learned task capabilities to solve the same tasks in the target domain, without relearning from scratch  (Fig. 1)  . To substantiate this idea, we analyze why one-shot fine-tuned models fail to adapt. Through subspace alignment analysis, we find that their parameter changes from base model, i.e., update-vectors, are predominantly task-specific, with small domain-specific directions present. From this, we further study how these directions coexist. We conjecture that the task and domain directions are additively composable, inspired by the disentangled weights for each task in vision-language models [20, 47, 73], and we find consistent results supporting this structural property in VLA model. Building on this observation, we propose Domain ARiThmetic (DART), an analogy-based framework for VLA adaptation inspired by weight arithmetic [20, 58,76]. Similar to queen = king + woman - man, we add domain vector that captures the environmental shift to the base model, transferring its multi-task capabilities to the target domain without additional data or architectural changes. To isolate the domain vector, we subtract the source-domain update-vector from the target-domain update-vector to cancel out shared task-specific directions, where each update-vector is fine-tuned on one-shot data of the same task. However, direct subtraction can retain source-domain artifacts and amplify fine-tuning noise [66, 68, 71], corrupting the extracted domain vector. To address this, motivated by the low-rank structure of update-vectors and subspace aligning property for relevant features in model merging [16,43,56], we introduce subspace Domain Arithmetic: One-Shot VLA Adaptation under Environmental Shifts 3 filtering, which filters misaligned subspace basis vectors between source and target updates for subtraction, and subspace scaling, which down-weights noisy domain vectors based on source-target subspace alignment. In simulated and real-world experiments with π0.5 [4] and π0-FAST [50], DART outperforms existing VLA adaptation baselines under one-shot scenarios across diverse visual and embodiment shifts. Moreover, our approach enables fast, hyperparameter-robust adaptation and merging across multiple target domains. Our contributions are as follows: (i) Empirical evidence shows that oneshot fine-tuned weights decompose into shareable, additive taskand domainspecific directions. (ii) From this observation, we propose DART that extracts reusable domain vector from one-shot fine-tuned weights by removing task-specific directions through an analogy operation. (iii) Our approach outperforms prior VLA adaptation methods across diverse simulated and real-world experiments.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Domain adaptation for Vision-Language-Action models. By integrating pretrained vision-language backbones [2, 38] with large-scale robotic datasets [25, 48,60], VLA models such as RT-2 [80], OpenVLA [26,27], and the π series [4,5,21, 50] have shown strong performance in wide range of tasks. However, they often require adaptation in novel environments to avoid performance degradation [13,33, 63,65,77]. common VLA adaptation approach trains with diverse augmentations to improve generalization [6,13,32,69], but it relies on fine-tuning with additional training data, incurring prohibitive data collection costs. Some approaches reduce this data burden by leveraging semantic-rich visual features [12,35,46] or introduce architectural modifications [1,14,63]. Yet these choices limit generalizability across backbones and deployment setups. Test-time adaptation methods [9, 40] adapt at inference time without extra VLA training, but are mainly targeting limited shift regimes. To address these limitations, we propose an architecture-agnostic VLA adaptation method with minimal target-domain data under diverse shifts. Analogy operation using weight arithmetic. Task Arithmetic (TA) [20] manipulates models using weight-space arithmetic operations, primarily through merging (i.e., addition) to compose capabilities, and analogy (i.e., queen - king = woman - man) to estimate parameter changes that transfer target properties. While merging has advanced through interference mitigation [8, 57, 66, 68] and subspace alignment [16,43,49,56,62], analogy remains limited to direct subtraction, applied in language models for cross-lingual adaptation [10, 76] and humanalignment transfer [19, 58]. In VLA models, existing work focuses exclusively on merging to improve generalization [12, 67, 70], or compose skills [14, 29, 61]. However, merging cannot selectively transfer specific capabilities such as domain knowledge while preserving others. This limitation motivates revisiting analogy for efficient VLA adaptation. Our empirical analysis reveals disentangled, additive taskand domain-specific directions in one-shot fine-tuned VLA models, making analogy natural fit for isolating domain vector. 4 T. Kang et al.

sections.title: 3 Preliminaries
sections.content: VLA fine-tuning. Let πθ(atot, ) denote Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policy parameterized by θ, which maps an observation ot (e.g., third-person and wrist camera images) and task instruction (e.g., language prompts) to distribution over actions at at time step t. Let Esrc and Etgt represent the source and target domains, respectively, where Etgt introduces environmental shifts (e.g., camera viewpoint or embodiment changes) in single (or small number of) environment of Esrc. We assume access to base policy θ0 that has been trained to solve suite of policy tasks = {T1, . . . , TM } within Esrc. For the target domain Etgt, we are given dataset Dm,tgt = {(otgt , at, Tm)} comprising single demonstration for one adaptation task Tm collected per environment in Etgt. Our objective is to produce adapted parameters θ such that πθ performs well in Etgt across all tasks in , despite observing only one-shot, single-task supervision Dm,tgt per environment. For adapting VLA models, we consider the adaptation through behavior cloning (BC) fine-tuning [5, 27, 80]. Initializing with θ0, we obtain the target-domain fine-tuned parameters θm,tgt by minimizing BC objective over the actions in Dm,tgt. We then evaluate θm,tgt within Etgt across all tasks in , including Tm and remaining held-out tasks Tk=m . Update-vector. To understand the property of fine-tuned weights, we analyze how the model changes through optimization. Building upon Task Arithmetic [20], we represent an adaptation as parameter change. Let θ(l) m,tgt Rdoutdin denote the weights of layer in the base and fine-tuned models, respectively. We define the target-domain update-vector in layer l, (l) 0 and θ(l) m,tgt, as: (l) m,tgt = θ(l) m,tgt θ(l) 0 , (1) and denote the full update-vector by m,tgt = {(l) m,tgt}L l=1.

sections.title: 4 Analysis of One-shot Fine-tuning Failures
sections.content: Given the substantial cost of collecting data across tasks in each new environment, we consider one-shot adaptation using single demonstration Dm,tgt for one adaptation task Tm in the target domain Etgt. We conjecture in 1 that Dm,tgt can provide transferable domain knowledge, enabling the base model θ0 to harness its multi-task capabilities in Etgt. natural approach is to fine-tune θ0 on Dm,tgt, yielding θm,tgt. However, on the LIBERO [37] benchmark with π0.5 [4], Fig. 2a shows that one-shot fine-tuning generally fails on held-out tasks Tk=m when evaluated in Etgt, where tgt = Medium (viewpoint shift relative to the source domain, see 6 for details). At the same time, performance of θm,tgt on Tm remains higher than that on Tk=m when evaluated in the source domain (i.e., Source viewpoint). This indicates that one-shot model updates primarily capture task-specific behavior rather than adapting to the target domain across tasks. sections.title: 4.2 Additive Composition of One-shot Update-Vector
sections.content: Building on observation of task and domain-shared directions in update-vectors in 4.1, we investigate how these directions coexist. Prior work shows that finetuning vision and language models for different tasks often updates orthogonal [73], disentangled weight subspaces [20, 24, 47], enabling interference-free additive composition of task capabilities. Because VLA models are built upon these pretrained vision-language backbones [4, 27], we expect them to inherit these structural properties. We further conjecture that this disentanglement extends beyond task capabilities to domain knowledge. Specifically, we hypothesize that within one-shot target-domain update-vector m,tgt, the directions capturing task-specific behavior and those capturing domain-specific adaptation can be independently identified and additively composed without mutual interference. Validation of additive composition via prototypes. We empirically validate the additive composition hypothesis. Suppose we obtain set of one-shot update-vectors m,tgt = θm,tgt θ0 from set of tasks and set of domains E. From this set of update-vectors, we define three prototypes: (i) task prototype := 1 tgt m,tgt averaged across domains, (ii) domain protoE (cid:80) type tgt := 1 m,tgt averaged across tasks, and (iii) global prototype := 1 m,tgt m,tgt. We expect that an update-vector for any specific taskdomain pair (m, tgt) should be correctly estimated via the additive composition as (cid:98)m,tgt := + tgt . Intuitively, and tgt capture domain-invariant E (cid:80) (cid:80) Domain Arithmetic: One-Shot VLA Adaptation under Environmental Shifts 7 task directions and task-agnostic domain directions, respectively, and subtracting removes common shifts inherent in fine-tuning. We evaluate how well each prototype and composition explains m,tgt using the subspace alignment metric γ(, m,tgt) in Eq. (2). In Fig. 3a, the alignment scores of each prototype across 16 update-vectors exhibit low standard deviation, implying consistent task and domain directions in prototypes and thus in each update-vector. Moreover, the composed estimate (cid:98)m,tgt yields the highest alignment with m,tgt, suggesting that domain and task adaptations map to linearly combinable directions in weight space, validating our hypothesis. We further discuss why the task and domain components are linearly decomposable in the supplementary material. Alignment between update-vectors on different domains. To further understand the properties of the domain directions in update-vectors, we keep the adaptation task fixed and evaluate the alignment of update-vectors across diverse target domains (e.g., viewpoint shifts of varying magnitude, camera noise, and their compositions) from LIBERO-Plus [13] benchmark. The subspace alignment results in Fig. 3b reveal that the domain-specific updates learned from fine-tuning are not arbitrary, but structured. Similar environmental changes (e.g., viewpoint shifts) produce similar update directions, while combining two shifts (e.g., viewpoint + noise) produces an update that partially reuses the directions learned for each shift alone. This suggests the model organizes domain knowledge in compositional way, where each type of environment change corresponds to distinct, reusable direction in weight space.

sections.title: 5 DART: Domain ARiThmetic
sections.content: In 4, we find that one-shot fine-tuned model fails to adapt since its updatevector is dominated by task-relevant directions, but we also find that the updatevector can be linearly decomposable into common task and domain components. Motivated by this, we aim to decompose and utilize the domain component for model adaptation. Therefore, we propose Domain ARiThmetic (DART), an analogy-based method inspired by weight arithmetic [20,76]. Instead of computing the domain prototype as in 4.2, which requires multiple target-domain tasks, we extract the domain direction from single target update-vector by removing task directions using source-domain demonstration. To further enhance this extraction, we introduce subspace filtering and scaling that remove domainirrelevant noise from update-vectors. Fig. 4 provides an overview of our approach.

sections.title: 5.1 Domain Vector Extraction
sections.content: Building on the additive properties in 4.2, we extract the domain-specific directions by subtracting the task-specific directions from the target update-vector m,tgt. To estimate these task directions without additional target-domain data, we leverage source-domain demonstration Dm,src of the same task Tm, which is typically available from the data used to train the base model θ0. sections.title: 5.2 Subspace Alignment for Enhanced Domain Vector
sections.content: The domain vector extraction through direct subtraction between target and source update-vectors may successfully isolate target-domain knowledge. However, fine-tuning often includes task-irrelevant noise [66, 68, 71], and even minor sourcedomain artifacts can be encoded in m,src. Because weight updates reside in lowrank subspaces that can be rotated or misaligned by fine-tuning artifacts [43,53,75], naive subtraction may inadvertently inject source-domain noise into the target domain vector or fail to remove task semantics. To remove irrelevant noise in domain vectors, we leverage the spectral properties in one-shot update-vectors. Subspace filtering. Our intuition is that the shared task semantics lie in the mutually shared subspace between m,src and m,tgt. By filtering the basis vectors of m,src that weakly align with m,tgt, we can prevent source-specific noise from corrupting the domain vector. We only filter the source update-vector as unique bases of target update-vector likely encode the domain directions we seek to isolate. sections.title: 6 Experiments
sections.content: To evaluate our method, we conduct (i) simulation experiments under diverse visual shifts and cross-embodiment transfer ( 6.2), (ii) real-world experiments under viewpoint shifts ( 6.3), and (iii) additional analyses ( 6.4).

sections.title: 6.1 Setups
sections.content: Models. We primarily evaluate our approach on π0.5 [4], flow-matching-based VLA model [36]. To assess architectural generality, we additionally evaluate on π0-FAST [50], which uses autoregressive action-token generation. Baselines. In both simulation and real-world experiments, we compare DART with architecture-agnostic VLA adaptation baselines: (i) Zero-shot (no adaptation), (ii) One-shot FT (full fine-tuning on the one-shot dataset), (iii) FLA [32] (vision encoder adaptation using LoRA [18]), and (iv) RETAIN [67] (model merging between source model and One-shot FT with module-wise scaling). Simulation setup. For visual shifts, we evaluate on LIBERO [37], robot manipulation benchmark with four task suites of total 40 tasks. Following prior work [13, 32, 63], we apply third-person viewpoint shifts and visual perturbations to LIBERO to mimic real-world environmental shifts (Fig. 5 left). We consider three levels of viewpoint shift relative to the source camera pose: Small, Medium, and Large, and two visual perturbations applied on top of the viewpoint shifts: Noise (noise injection) and Light (illumination change). We adapt the base model trained on the original LIBERO training dataset [4, 50] to each target domain using scene-wise one-shot dataset collected in the target domain, containing single demonstration from one task in each of the five LIBERO scenes. We repeat one-shot adaptation three times with different randomly selected adaptation tasks and report the average Success Rate (%), with 50 rollouts per task. sections.title: 6.2 Simulation Results
sections.content: Novel visual domains. As shown in Tab. 1, DART outperforms all baselines under diverse novel viewpoints. Notably, applying the domain vector δtgt to the base policy (θ0) yields substantial gain of 24.6 percentage points (pp). These results support our hypothesis that domain vectors provide domain-specific knowledge while preserving multi-task capabilities. Our method outperforms FLA [32], which fine-tunes only the vision encoder, highlighting the importance of adapting the entire model in data-limited setting. Compared with RETAIN [67], our analogy-based approach performs better, suggesting that explicitly isolating domain-shift directions helps under data scarcity. Tab. 2 summarizes performance under combined visual perturbations. DART maintains clear advantage over baselines across all settings. This trend indicates that the advantage of our method persists even under combined environmental shifts, rather than being limited to single perturbation type. Applicability to an alternative VLA architecture. To test generalizability beyond π0.5s flow-matching formulation, we apply our approach to π0-FAST, an autoregressive VLA model. As shown in Tab. 3, our method also consistently outperforms all baselines on this architecture. This result supports that the additive structure of update-vectors is applicable to diverse VLA architectures. Beyond visual domain shifts, upgrading hardware or transferring policies across different robotic platforms remains major bottleneck in real-world deployment. To test if our approach can bridge this physical domain gap, we examine whether the analogy principle in Eq. (3) extends to cross-embodiment transfer. Tab. 4 shows that DART is also applicable to the cross-embodiment setting, which differs substantially from visual domain adaptation. This highlights that our approach can be applied across diverse visual and physical environmental shifts without any algorithmic modification.

sections.title: 6.3 Real-world Results
sections.content: We evaluate DART in real-world setup to assess whether it remains effective under real-world variability. Table 5 summarizes results under third-person camera viewpoint shifts on UR10e robot. Despite adapting from only single Stack Cube demonstration, our method achieves high success rates across all five tasks, indicating that their domain-level transfer is effective even in diverse real-world conditions. In contrast, baselines perform substantially worse under the same one-shot budget, indicating limited transfer beyond the demonstration. We provide experiment videos in the supplementary material.

sections.title: 6.4 Detailed Analysis
sections.content: Ablation study. Tab. 6 isolates the effect of each component in DART. DART without any subspace alignment component yields substantial improvement over One-shot FT (in Tab. 1), suggesting that the analogy-based weight arithmetic between sourceand target-domain update vectors effectively isolates domainspecific knowledge. This validates the predominant, domain-agnostic task-specific directions among update vectors evidenced in 4.1, and confirms their linearly decomposable property consistent with 4.2. Building on this, subspace filtering leads to marked improvement, highlighting the importance of suppressing noisy source-domain artifacts in the source-domain update vector for more precise domain vector extraction. Furthermore, subspace scaling yields additional gains, suggesting the presence of highly misaligned, low-quality domain vectors whose contribution is better modulated through alignment-aware reweighting. 14 T. DART reports average success rates (%) of separately adapted model in three novel viewpoints in LIBERO. Each single merged model is evaluated across the three domains. Components Sub. Filter. Sub. (b) Impact of fine-tuning steps. Fig. 6: Performance under hyperparameter choices on LIBERO across novel viewpoints. We average success rates (%) across Small, Medium, Large camera views. Merging multiple domain vectors. We study whether domain vectors for different target domains from DART can be consolidated into single transferable vector, motivated by the additivity of domain directions in weight space ( 4.2). We merge three novel viewpoint-shift domain vectors δtgt from LIBERO into combined vector δ using model-merging methods [16, 20, 43, 66], and adapt the base model as θ = θ0 + α δ. As shown in Tab. 7, the merged vector successfully adapts the base model to all three domains, emphasizing the composable nature of domain vectors. This indicates the practicality of DART that only single consolidated vector can be maintained across multiple target domains, reducing the memory overhead of storing each domain vector. Scaling coefficient α. Figure 6a shows how DART and its simplified version, DART without Subspace Alignment (DART w/o SA) that does not apply subspace filtering or scaling, vary in performance with the scaling coefficient α in Eq. (7). Small α is insufficient to address the environmental shift, whereas large α can interfere with the base policys multi-task capabilities. Nevertheless, DART maintains strong performance across wide range of α. In particular, DART is more stable than DART w/o SA, suggesting that subspace filtering and scaling suppress noisy components that would otherwise be amplified by α. Domain Arithmetic: One-Shot VLA Adaptation under Environmental Shifts Fine-tuning steps. Fig. 6b shows the effect of the number of fine-tuning steps for the update-vectors in Eq. (3). While One-shot FT degrades over time due to catastrophic forgetting [67], DART, which extracts the domain vector from One-shot FT weights, shows small but consistent performance gains, likely as task-specific directions become more pronounced with training, leading to better domain vector extraction. Notably, DART maintains strong performance even at small fine-tuning steps, enabling time-efficient adaptation. We further show that DART avoids source-domain forgetting, compare DART with existing model-merging and test-time-adaptation methods, and analyze the choice of layers to which we add the domain vector and the choice of tasks used to extract it. Please see the supplementary material for details.

sections.title: 7 Limitation
sections.content: While DART consistently outperforms baselines, performance degrades under severe shifts (e.g., Large viewpoint), challenge shared by all one-shot methods. We leave this to future work, e.g., via more reliable domain vector extraction or stronger base-model training/fine-tuning schemes. Additionally, the scalar coefficient α requires small hyperparameter search, though our analysis shows DART remains stable across wide range of values and generalizes well across environments. Hyperparameter-free per-layer adaptive scaling for practical realworld application is left for future work.

sections.title: 8 Conclusion
sections.content: We propose method to adapt VLA models for environmental shifts with only single demonstration collection. Motivated by our observation that one-shot finetuned parameters admit an approximately additive decomposition into taskand domain-specific directions, we introduce DART, an analogy-based approach that adds filtered domain-specific directions isolated by weight arithmetic. Extensive evaluation in simulation and on real-world setups shows consistent improvement and applicability of DART across diverse visual and embodiment shifts.

sections.title: Supplementary Material
sections.content: This supplementary material provides additional technical details, experimental protocols, and extended analyses for DART. Specifically, we include: Method Details. Section A.1: Overall algorithm of DART. Section A.2: Justification for using source-domain demonstrations. Section A.3: Acceleration of DART with randomized SVD. Section A.4: Motivation for taskdomain decomposition in update-vectors. Baseline Details. Section B.1: Implementation details of RETAIN. Section B.2: Implementation details of FLA. Experimental Setup. Section C.1: VLA model and training hyperparameters. Section C.2: LIBERO setup for visual domain shifts. Section C.3: MimicGen setup for cross-embodiment transfer. Section C.4: Real-world robot setup. Additional Results and Analysis. Section D.1: Suite-wise LIBERO results. Section D.2.: Full-data fine-tuning upper-bound results. Section D.3: Comparison with model merging methods. Section D.4: Comparison with test-time adaptation. Section D.5: Further analysis of DART. Details of DART A.1 Algorithm We summarize the procedure of our analogy-based proposed method, Domain ARiThmetic (DART), in Algorithm 1. A.2 Justification for Using Source-domain Demonstrations key assumption in DART is access to the source-domain training dataset used to pretrain the source policy, along with at least one source-domain demonstration for the adaptation task Tm. Below we explain why this assumption is plausible and how it can be relaxed. Availability of source-domain demonstrations. In many robotics settings, pretrained policies are trained on large-scale open-source robotic datasets [25, 48], from which we can retrieve demonstrations for adaptation. Also, since recent VLA models commonly rely on task-wise fine-tuning on teleoperated demonstrations to perform multiple tasks in given environment [4, 26, 27], it is natural that the demonstrations used for such fine-tuning are available as source data. Moreover, our method requires only small number of source-domain demonstrations (e.g., single trajectory) to compute m,src, rather than access to the full dataset. 22 T. Kang et al. We report success rate (%) and runtime on novel viewpoints. For randomized SVD, we use target rank of r=256. Runtime is averaged over three runs and measured on machine with 1TB RAM and an Intel Xeon Platinum 8562Y+ CPU. Novel Viewpoints (Success Rate, %) Method (π0.5) DART + Full SVD 92.0 DART + Randomized SVD 91.7 Small Medium Large Average Runtime 80.8 80.7 64.4 63.8 79.1 78.7 15m 35s 6m 33s Obtaining the same adaptation task across domains. The assumption that we can identify the same task Tm in the source dataset is straightforward when the source training set contains limited and well-defined task taxonomy (as in standard benchmarks). When the source dataset is large or weakly organized, an exact task lookup may be difficult. However, this does not prevent our approach in practice: we can instead choose the adaptation task from the source side first. Concretely, we sample source-domain demonstration from the training set, treat its underlying task as Tm, and then collect target-domain expert demonstration for the same task. This simple protocol guarantees the same adaptation task by construction, avoiding the need for explicit task indexing in the source dataset. Robustness to imperfect task matching. Even when exact matching is not possible (e.g., when we can only collect expert demonstration on certain tasks), our method remains usable. In Tab. 22, we show that using source update-vector from different task (m = m) degrades performance, but selecting similar task (via feature cosine similarity) consistently outperforms random choice. This suggests that approximate task matching can still yield meaningful domain vectors, and that performance can further improve with stronger task retrieval mechanisms [11, 28, 64] and with more diverse source training sets [25, 48, 60]. Takeaway. Overall, requiring source-domain demonstration is mild and practical assumption: it can be satisfied either by direct access to source training data, or by selecting the adaptation task from the source dataset and collecting the corresponding target-domain demonstration. When only approximate matches are available, similarity-based retrieval provides viable fallback with room for improvement. A.3 Accelerating DART with Randomized SVD Our method is computationally lightweight compared to training-based adaptation baselines [13, 32], as it only requires few one-shot fine-tuning runs followed by weight-space arithmetic. However, DART involves computing Singular Value Decompositions (SVD) of both base and target update vectors at each layer, which can be computationally expensive for larger models. To reduce this overhead, we replace full SVD with truncated randomized SVD approximation [17] T. Kang et al. (Randomized SVD), which estimates the top-r singular subspace without performing full decomposition. This reduces the dominant per-layer cost from O(mn min(m, n)) (full SVD) to approximately O(mnr) for an matrix with target rank min(m, n), where and denote the output and input dimensions of the layer. As shown in Tab. 8, Randomized SVD achieves comparable performance to full SVD while substantially reducing computation. A.4 Why Do One-Shot Update-Vectors Decompose into Task and Domain-Specific Directions Intuitively, VLA inputs contain distinct but overlapping token subsets that are mainly associated with either task information (e.g., language instructions and task-relevant objects) or domain information (e.g., background appearance, camera viewpoint, and robot embodiment). Thus, one-shot fine-tuning can induce partially disentangled weight directions [47]. To empirically inspect this property, we add each prototype update-vector (Sec. 4.2) to the base model θ0 and measure last-layer token feature shiftsthe L2 distance between features before and after adding the prototypeacross different token types. As shown in Tab. 9 and Fig. 7, the Task prototype predominantly shifts text and task-relevant object tokens, while the Domain prototype predominantly shifts background tokens. These results are consistent with recent findings that distinct tasks activate separable column subspaces of weight matrices and that weight interpolation affects intermediate features and functional outputs approximately linearly in pretrained models (based on NTK theory) [39, 78], together implying that taskand domain-specific directions can be approximately decomposed and recomposed in weight space. Furthermore, it motivates DARTs core operation of subtracting m,src from m,tgt to isolate the target-domain direction δtgt. sections.title: B Details on Baseline Methods
sections.content: B.1 RETAIN [67] RETAIN is parameter merging method for VLA models that enables learning new task while mitigating forgetting of previously learned tasks. It interpolates Domain Arithmetic: One-Shot VLA Adaptation under Environmental Shifts 25 Table 10: Verification of FLA implementation. Comparison between the success rates (%) reported in [32] and our reproduced results under the same experimental setup. Novel Viewpoints (Success Rate, %) Method (π0.5) FLA (Reported) FLA (Reproduced) Small Medium Large Average 94.6 96. 90.0 90.8 87.9 87.9 90.8 91.6 between the original model parameters and the fine-tuned parameters, balancing knowledge acquisition from the fine-tuned model with retention of the original multi-task capability. In our experiments, we treat target-domain task as the new task. Specifically, we first obtain one-shot fine-tuned model using the same setting as ours, and then merge its parameters with the original source model following RETAIN. For the scaling coefficient α, we independently sweep the coefficients applied to the vision encoder, LLM, and action expert modules, and report the bestperforming combination. The selected coefficients are 0.6, 0.4, and 0.2 for the vision, LLM, and action expert modules, respectively. B.2 FLA [32] FLA is parameter-efficient method for adapting VLA models to new domains. It achieves strong performance by inserting LoRA [18] layers into the vision encoder while freezing the remaining model parameters. It is originally designed to adapt VLA models to new environment using task-wise one-shot demonstrations. Since we consider more restrictive data-limited setting, we apply FLA under the same scene-wise one-shot protocol as our method. Specifically, we use only one demonstration per scene (i.e., the total number of demonstrations equals the number of scenes), rather than one demonstration per task as originally done in FLA. This ensures fair comparison under an identical data budget. We reproduce FLA following the implementation details described in the paper [32]. To verify correctness, we evaluate our implementation under the experimental protocol reported in the original paper (i.e., using task-wise oneshot demonstrations). Our reproduced model achieves performance consistent with the reported results, as summarized in Table 10.

sections.content: C.1 VLA Model and Training Hyperparameter Details We use two VLA models, π0.5 [4] and π0-FAST [50]. All training and evaluation are conducted using the official openpi codebase3, implemented in JAX. We use the default model architectures without architectural modifications. We use AdamW [41] with batch size 64 for all training runs. Training hyperparameters are summarized in Tab. 11; unless specified in the table, we follow the default settings of the openpi repository. C.2 LIBERO Setup Details This section describes the detailed experimental setup on LIBERO [37] for novel viewpoint shifts and combined visual perturbation experiments. We use the official LIBERO codebase4 with minor modifications to implement domain shifts. Training and datasets. The LIBERO dataset comprises four task suites (Spatial, Object, Goal, Long), each containing 10 tasks with 50 demonstrations per task (2,000 demonstrations in total). These tasks are distributed across five scenes: Living Room, Kitchen, Floor, Study, and Tabletop  (Fig. 8)  . Specifically, the Spatial and Goal suites are set in the Tabletop scene, Object uses the Floor scene, and Long spans the Living Room, Kitchen, and Study scenes. We use the filtered version of LIBERO dataset5, as in OpenVLA [27]. As our multi-task source π0.5 model, we adopt the checkpoint trained on the four LIBERO task suites available in openpi6. For action normalization, we use the statistics provided with the checkpoint and keep them fixed for all subsequent fine-tuning and evaluation. Since no official π0-FAST checkpoint pretrained on LIBERO is available, we train the π0-FAST source model from pi0_fast_base7 on four NVIDIA H100 GPUs. For one-shot fine-tuning both of π0.5 and π0-FAST, we use single demonstration from single task in each of the five scenes. As the one-shot demo, we take the first trajectory in each task dataset. In the target domain, the one-shot 4 https://github.com/Lifelong-Robot-Learning/LIBERO 5 https://huggingface.co/datasets/openvla/modified_libero_rlds 6 gs://openpi-assets/checkpoints/pi05_libero 7 gs://openpi-assets/checkpoints/pi0_fast_base Domain Arithmetic: One-Shot VLA Adaptation under Environmental Shifts 27 Fig. 8: Images of each LIBERO scene under different viewpoints. Columns represent viewpoint shift levels, and rows correspond to scenes. fine-tuning uses the regenerated dataset collected under the target domain shift. We use three different combinations of adaptation tasks for one-shot training and report the average success rate evaluated over all tasks. The combinations are shown in Tab. 13. Fine-tuning is performed on two NVIDIA A100 GPUs. Evaluation. For each task within suite, we execute 50 rollout trials using the default initial states provided by the benchmark (10 tasks 50 rollouts = 500 rollouts per suite). An episode is considered successful if the environment returns done signal (i.e., the task completion condition is met) within the allotted horizon. The maximum episode horizon is set per suite based on the longest demonstration in the corresponding training set: 220 steps for Spatial, 280 for Object, 300 for Goal, and 520 for Long. At the start of each episode, we execute 10 no-op steps (zero translation/rotation with gripper open) to allow objects to settle in the simulator before control begins. We tune the scaling coefficient α 28 T. Kang et al. Table 12: Rotation angles for viewpoint shifts in LIBERO across scenes. Since the default camera pose differs across scenes, the resulting rotation angles vary by scene. All values are reported in degrees. Scene Living Room Kitchen Study Floor Tabletop Camera Rotation Angle () Small Medium Large 26.3 24.5 33.2 18.5 24.5 59.9 56.8 69.7 45.1 56.8 120.7 118.4 126.6 106.9 118.4 on small set (10 rollouts) in the Long task suite and reuse this value across all tasks in the same setting. We use α = 0.8 for viewpoint shifts and α = 0.6 for visual perturbations. We report the Success Rate, defined as the fraction of trials in which the task is completed, averaged over all tasks within each suite and then averaged across all LIBERO suites. We use single NVIDIA A100 GPU for inference and run all experiments in Docker containers to ensure consistent software environment. Implementation of Visual Domain Shifts. We modify the camera position and orientation in LIBERO to construct novel viewpoints, following prior work [32, 63]. Viewpoint shifts are defined as translational offsets of the camera in the MuJoCo [59] simulator. Specifically, we define three levels of viewpoint shifts, applied relative to the default camera position: Small: (0.0, +0.3, 0.1) Medium: (0.2, +0.7, 0.2) Large: (1.2, +1.0, 0.2) After applying the translation, we rotate the camera to look at the initial endeffector position at (0.0, 0.0, 0.0). Because the default camera pose differs across scenes, the resulting rotation angles also vary by scene. The full rotation angles are provided in Table 12 and preview images are shown in Figure 8. For Light perturbations, we modify the illumination by increasing the bluechannel intensity in both diffuse and specular components while attenuating the red and green channels. In addition, we reposition the light sources to single centralized overhead location at lower height. We simulate Noise by applying Gaussian blur (27 27 kernel, σ 4.4) to all observation images at every timestep, substantially degrading high-frequency visual information such as object edges and textures. C.3 MimicGen Setup Details This section provides dataset, training, and evaluation details for cross-embodiment transfer on MimicGen [42]. We use the official MimicGen codebase8 for all experiments. Figure 9 shows example rollouts of the evaluation tasks. Training and datasets. We pretrain π0.5 on two MimicGen tasks (Square and Threading) on both embodiments under the D0 object randomization setting to obtain stable initialization for our cross-embodiment experiments. We use 3,800 demonstrations for this pretraining stage, all generated using the official MimicGen data generation pipeline. We then train the source model θsrc on the Panda robot using the Stack and Stack Three datasets under the D0 object initialization (1,900 demonstrations in total). One-shot fine-tuning is conducted on the Stack task using single demonstration from the Panda dataset and single demonstration from the UR5e dataset; in both cases, we use the first generated trajectory. We compute action normalization statistics from the Square/Threading pretraining data and keep them fixed for all subsequent training and evaluation. All models are trained on two NVIDIA A100 GPUs. Evaluation. We apply the same action normalization statistics computed in Sec. C.3 for all evaluations. The episode time limit is 200 steps for Stack and 400 steps for Stack Three. The task descriptions for each task are: 8 https://github.com/NVlabs/mimicgen Domain Arithmetic: One-Shot VLA Adaptation under Environmental Shifts 31 Fig. 10: Real-world setup and example rollouts. Left: Viewpoint configuration for real-world experiments, using one wrist-mounted camera and third-person camera (source or target viewpoint). Right: Example rollout of the Lemon task from the source viewpoint (top) and the target viewpoint (bottom). Stack: stack the red cube on the green cube Stack Three: stack the red cube on the green cube, then stack the blue cube on the red cube We report both Progress Rate and Success Rate. For Progress Rate, we compute the maximum milestone reached within an episode based on the rubric in Tab. 14, and average it across evaluation rollouts. As in LIBERO (Sec. C.2), we execute 10 no-op steps to stabilize the environment. We set α = 0.4, tuned with 10 rollouts on Stack using held-out seed. All experiments are conducted on single NVIDIA A6000 GPU. C.4 Real-world Setup Details This section outlines details on real-world experiments. Throughout our experiments, we use single UR10e arm with Robotiq 2F-85 gripper. For vision, we use three RealSense D455 cameras: one wrist-mounted camera and two fixed third-person cameras corresponding to the Source and Target viewpoints, respectively. Figure 10 shows the viewpoint configuration and example rollouts in both viewpoints. Tasks. As described in the main paper, we consider three pick-and-place tasks and two fine-grained manipulation tasks. The pick-and-place task descriptions are: Eggplant: put the eggplant in the bowl Carrot: put the carrot on the towel Lemon: put the lemon on the plate The fine-grained manipulation task descriptions are: Stack Cube: stack the red cube on the green cube Press Stapler: press the stapler We use the same fixed language instruction for each task across training and evaluation. For rollout examples, please see the supplementary video. T. Kang et al. Table 15: Real-world performance of the base π0.5 policy on UR10e robot before adaptation. We report success rates (%) over 12 object positions per task. Source evaluates the base policy under the Source viewpoint, and Target evaluates the same base policy under the Target viewpoint in zero-shot manner. Success Rate (%) Pick-and-Place Fine-grained Manipulation Viewpoint Source Target (Zero-shot) Eggplant Lemon Carrot Stack Cube Press Stapler Average 100.0 16.7 100.0 50.0 100.0 75.0 100.0 41.7 98.3 43. 91.7 33.3 Training and datasets. In this experiment, we first train the source policy θ0 on demonstrations from all five tasks to obtain multi-task policy, and then perform one-shot fine-tuning for model analogy. We predefine 12 object positions for each task. For source-policy training, we collect 120 teleoperated demonstrations (24 per task; 2 demonstrations per position) using Meta Quest 3 [22]. For one-shot fine-tuning, we collect single target-domain demonstration for Stack Cube under the Target viewpoint, and use the corresponding sourcedomain demonstration from the same predefined position. We compute action normalization statistics from the source-policy training dataset and reuse them for all subsequent training and evaluation. Source-model training uses four NVIDIA A100 GPUs, and one-shot fine-tuning uses two NVIDIA A100 GPUs. To verify the feasibility of our real-world task setups, we evaluate the base policy θ0 in the source domain. As shown in Table 15, the base policy achieves near-perfect success rates on all tasks in the source domain. Evaluation. We evaluate each policy on the same 12 predefined object positions used during training. While each model inference predicts the next 20 actions, we execute only the first 15 actions on the real robot. All experiments are conducted on single NVIDIA A6000 GPU.

sections.title: D Additional Experiment Results
sections.content: D.1 Detailed LIBERO Results by Task Suite We report suite-wise success rates on LIBERO (Spatial, Object, Goal, and Long) in Tab. 23, Tab. 24, and Tab. 25. Overall, our method outperforms baselines across most task suites and domain-shift settings, indicating consistent improvements. Interestingly, π0-FAST (Tab. 25) achieves higher zero-shot success than π0.5 (Tab. 23) under viewpoint shifts, aligning with [13]. Since continuous regression can suffer from compounding feature drift [30,54,55], we speculate that π0-FASTs discrete classification acts as an implicit regularizer, enhancing robustness to visual shifts. Setting Adaptation Type Task # Demos Success Rate (%) LIBERO Novel Viewpoint Small Medium Large Average 1,716 1,716 1,716 1,716 87.1 87.9 86.0 87.0 D.2 Upper Bound Performance of Adaptation Although our approach focuses on an one-shot, single-task adaptation, fully fine-tuning on sufficiently large target-domain dataset is expected to yield the best achievable performance in the target domain. We use this regime as an empirical upper bound. For each experimental setting, we fully fine-tune the base policy θ0 on the corresponding target-domain dataset for 10,000 steps. The amount of targetdomain data matches the scale used to train the base policy. For LIBERO [37], we regenerate the full training set for each target domain using all demonstrations after applying the OpenVLA filtering protocol [27] (50 demos per task 10 tasks 4 suites, yielding 1,716 demonstrations after filtering). For MimicGen [42], we generate 950 target-domain demonstrations per task following the standard MimicGen data generation procedure. 34 T. Kang et al. Table 18: Comparison with model merging methods under novel viewpoint shifts. Average success rates (%) on LIBERO across three viewpoint shifts (Small, Medium, Large), with the best in bold and the second best underlined. Method (π0.5) TIES [66] (NeurIPS 2023) Iso-C [43] (ICML 2025) RESM [68] (NeurIPS 2025) DART (Ours) Small Medium Large Average 91.9 91.8 90.8 92.0 79.8 76.4 78.0 80.8 61.0 55.3 57.7 64. 77.6 74.5 75.5 79.1 Tables 16 and 17 reports the resulting success rates and the number of demonstrations used for adaptation. As expected, full-data fine-tuning consistently yields strong success rate. However, it requires much more target-domain expert demonstrations, which is impractical at real-world deployment scenarios. Collecting diverse task-wise demonstrations across many environments is prohibitively expensive, which takes several days to collect and several hours to train. In contrast, DART can instantly improve performance in the target domain using only single demonstration per environment, highlighting its data efficiency. D.3 Comparison with Model Merging Methods We demonstrate that our proposed model analogy method, DART, shows superior performance over existing VLA adaptation methods. Here, we compare with existing model merging methods, Task Arithmetic [20] branch that merges multiple weights into single weight to combine the capabilities of those weights, to demonstrate that several knowledge interference mitigation approaches [43,66,68] are not appropriate to extract domain vector. Specifically, given the updatevectors m,tgt and m,src, we apply existing model merging methods to combine the two update-vectors [m,tgt, m,src], and we add the combined vector into the base model θ0 by scaling it with the coefficient α. We tune the coefficient α in the same way as in our main experiments. As shown in Tab. 18, recent state-of-the-art model merging methods show lower success rate compared to our model analogy based method. This suggests that interference-mitigation strategies for model merging are not directly applicable to model analogy, whose goal is to cancel shared components between source and target updates to isolate the transfer signal. D.4 Comparison with test-time-adaptation method. We compare DART with SCALE [9] using π0-FAST [50] on LIBERO [37] (Tab. 19). DART outperforms SCALE across all novel viewpoints, showing the necessity of VLA policy adaptation under environment shifts. D.5 Additional Detailed Analysis Source domain performance after adaptation. DART performs comparably to the original base policy (Zero-shot) in the source domain. This suggests that our model analogy framework mitigates forgetting caused by adaptation, indicating that the adapted model can be used across environments, in both source and target domains. Merging multiple domain vectors with detailed results. We investigate whether domain vectors estimated for different target domains can be consolidated into single vector that generalizes across multiple domains, motivated by our empirical finding that domain directions are relatively disentangled and approximately additive in weight space. Specifically, we take three DART domain vectors δtgt obtained from LIBERO under novel viewpoint shifts (Small, Medium, Large) and merge them into combined vector δ using recent model-merging techniques [16, 20, 43, 66, 68]. We then adapt the source model θ0 by adding the merged vector with scaling coefficient α, i.e., θ = θ0 + α δ. As shown in Tab. 21, the merged domain vector yields moderate and consistent success rates across all three target viewpoint domains, indicating that domainlevel updates can be composed into single transferable direction. This further supports the hypothesis that domain directions decompose (approximately) additively. Practically, it suggests that deployment can maintain one consolidated 36 T. Kang et al. Table 21: Performance of merging domain vectors δtgt from each novel viewpoint domain into single adapted model in LIBERO using π0.5. We report average success rates of total 40 tasks for three trials of one-shot adaptation with different adaptation tasks, with the best in bold and the second best underlined. Merging domain vectors into single domain vector can adapt the base model to all the target domains. In practice, this property can be used to store only single composed domain vector across multiple target domains, reducing the memory overhead of storing each domain vector. Novel Viewpoints (Success Rate, %) Small Medium Large Average Methods (π0.5) Zero-shot DART (tgt = Small) DART (tgt = Medium) DART (tgt = Large) DART 88.3 92.0 76.4 72. 92.0 63.9 52.0 80.8 56.7 80.8 Merging three domain vectors from DART TA [20] (ICLR 2023) TIES [66] (NeurIPS 2023) TSV [16] (CVPR 2025) Iso-C [43] (ICML 2025) RESM [68] (NeurIPS 2025) 91.3 91.7 90.6 91.4 91. 78.7 75.8 79.9 79.9 76.5 11.3 6.2 10.9 64.4 64.4 53.5 46.2 56.6 53.0 40.5 54.5 50.1 56.0 64.4 79. 74.5 70.9 75.7 74.8 69.4 domain vector for multiple domains, reducing the memory overhead compared to storing separate vector per target domain. Different adaptation task from source and target domains. So far, we extract the domain vector δtgt from the target update-vector m,tgt and the source update-vector m,src trained on demonstration of the same adaptation task Tm, i.e., m,tgt m,src. We further study the effect of using different source update-vector m,src trained on demonstration of different adaptation task Tm, i.e., δtgt = m,tgt m,src. To isolate the effect of the source-domain adaptation task, we choose Tm per scene using two strategies: (i) similar-task choice and (ii) random-task choice. Setup. For similar-task choice, we rank candidate tasks by feature-level similarity score computed from the last-layer hidden states of the LLM backbone of the VLA model. Results. As summarized in Tab. 22, we evaluate on LIBERO with total 40 tasks using the π0.5 model. We observe that performance is highest when the source and target adaptation tasks match (i.e., = m). This supports our hypothesis that update-vectors contain largely domain-agnostic task directions, so subtracting m,src from m,tgt more effectively cancels task-specific components and isolates the domain vector. When = m, selecting Tm via cosine-similarity retrieval consistently outperforms random selection. While the gain remains modestlikely due to LIBEROs limited task set and our simple retrieval heuristicthe trend suggests that task similarity is useful signal for domain-vector extraction. Importantly, this result is encouraging for realistic settings where the sourcedomain data are large and not cleanly indexed by task, making exact matches to the pre-defined adaptation task Tm from the target-domain unlikely. In such cases, retrieving demonstrations of similar tasks can still yield meaningful adaptation, and we expect further improvements with stronger retrieval methods [11, 28, 64] and with source datasets that contain more diverse tasks (as is typical in largescale real-world robot datasets [25, 48, 60], which include 527 distinct tasks [48]). 38 T. Similar to the subspace alignment score plot, MLP/Up_proj (and MLP/Gate_proj) layer exhibit consistently low average overlap energy, indicating that many subspace vectors in the sourcedomain update-vector are highly misaligned with those of the target domain. This can be explained by the functional role of MLP layers in transformers: the factual knowledge memorized by the model is stored in the MLP layers, where Up_proj generates the keys used to retrieve certain values in Down_proj [31, 44, 45]. If we apply this prior observation, the subspace vectors in Up_proj responsible for generating domain-specific keys are likely highly specialized to the source domain (as the VLA model is trained to look up domain-specific values in Down_proj), and thus exhibit low alignment with the corresponding target-domain subspace.
